name: Babia Valley Loop
name_original: El Tajo del Sil – circular por la comarca de Babia desde San Emiliano
description: This difficult 34.4-mile touring cycling route through the Babia Biosphere Reserve offers stunning mountain landscapes and varied terrain.
region_intro: Touring cycling routes around San Emiliano are set within the Babia y Luna Natural Park, a Biosphere Reserve in the Cantabrian Mountains. The region features dramatic landscapes with abrupt mountain reliefs, deep valleys, and distinctive Karstic rock formations. Extensive forests of beeches, oaks, and birches cover the San Emiliano canyon, providing varied terrain for cyclists. This area offers a network of routes through its natural features, including mountain passes and scenic valleys. Best touring cycling routes around San Emiliano The most popular touring cycling route is Babia Valley Loop, a 34.4 miles (55.3 km) trail that takes 3 hours 50 minutes to complete. This route offers varied terrain and scenic beauty through the Babia valley. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is Alto de la Farrapona – View of Lago de la Cueva loop from Torrestío, a difficult 29.8 miles (48.0 km) path. This route includes significant elevation gain and leads towards sights within the Somiedo Natural Park. Local touring cyclists also love the La Cubilla Pass loop from Rospaso, a 16.0 miles (25.8 km) trail leading through challenging mountain terrain, often completed in about 2 hours 10 minutes. Touring cycling around San Emiliano is defined by dramatic mountain reliefs, deep valleys, and extensive forests. The network offers options primarily for experienced cyclists, with 25 difficult routes available. The routes in San Emiliano are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.0 stars from 5 reviews. More than 200 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore San Emiliano's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many touring cycling routes are available in San Emiliano?
A: San Emiliano offers a network of over 25 touring cycling routes. The region is known for its challenging terrain, with 25 routes rated as difficult and one as moderate, making it ideal for experienced cyclists.

Q: What kind of landscapes can I expect to see while touring cycling in San Emiliano?
A: You'll experience dramatic landscapes characterized by abrupt mountain reliefs, deep valleys, and distinctive Karstic rock formations. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/176266" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ventana Pass&lt;/a&gt; and the majestic Peña Ubiña are prominent features. The San Emiliano canyon is richly forested with beeches, oaks, and birches, providing varied and scenic terrain.

Q: What is the best time of year for touring cycling in San Emiliano?
A: The most popular months for touring cycling in San Emiliano are July and August. During these months, the weather is generally favorable, allowing cyclists to fully enjoy the region's well-maintained roads and low traffic conditions.

Q: Are there any family-friendly cycling options in San Emiliano?
A: While many routes in San Emiliano are challenging, the broader region offers options like the Senda del Oso (Bear's Track). This greenway, originally a railway line, provides an interlinked series of cycle paths suitable for all experience levels, including families and intermediate cyclists, covering over 45 km through scenic valleys.

Q: What are some notable natural landmarks or viewpoints along the cycling routes?
A: Cyclists can encounter several impressive natural landmarks. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6333018" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Cubilla Pass&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/176266" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ventana Pass&lt;/a&gt; offer breathtaking vistas. You might also pass by the scenic Somiedo lakes, the Barrios de Luna Dam, and the Mirador del Puerto de Ventana, which provides expansive views of the surrounding landscapes.

Q: Are there any circular touring cycling routes in San Emiliano?
A: Yes, there are several circular routes. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1035955096/babia-valley-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Babia Valley Loop&lt;/a&gt; is a popular 55.3 km trail that offers varied terrain and scenic beauty. Another option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/10504191" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Cubilla Pass loop from Rospaso&lt;/a&gt;, a 25.8 km route through challenging mountain terrain.

Q: What do other cyclists enjoy most about touring cycling in San Emiliano?
A: The touring cycling routes in San Emiliano are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.0 stars from 5 reviews. Cyclists often praise the dramatic mountain scenery, the quiet roads, and the challenging climbs that offer rewarding views.

Q: Can I find routes that explore cultural or historical sites?
A: Yes, some routes allow you to explore the region's rich heritage. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31875707" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;San Emiliano – Palace of Quiñones loop from San Emiliano&lt;/a&gt; takes you past the Renaissance Quiñones palace in Riolago, and you can also spot emblazoned houses in various villages, testifying to a past of rural noblemen.

Q: What is the typical difficulty level for touring cycling routes in San Emiliano?
A: The majority of touring cycling routes in San Emiliano are considered difficult. Out of 26 available routes, 25 are rated as difficult, often featuring significant elevation gains and challenging mountain passes. There is one moderate route available for those seeking a slightly less strenuous ride.

Q: Are there any routes that lead to the Somiedo Natural Park?
A: Yes, from San Emiliano, cyclists can access routes that lead into the Somiedo Natural Park. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40007612" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Alto de la Farrapona – View of Lago de la Cueva loop from Torrestío&lt;/a&gt; is a difficult 48.0 km path that includes significant elevation gain and leads towards sights within the Somiedo Natural Park.

Q: What kind of wildlife might I encounter while cycling?
A: The varied vegetation and extensive forests in the Babia y Luna Natural Park support a diverse array of wildlife. While cycling, you might spot birds of prey, chamois, roe deer, deer, wild boars, and otters, especially in quieter areas.
tips: This has always been a place for nomadic vaqueiros who come from Asturias looking for pasture for their cattle through Puerto de la Ventana. Here the patsuezu variant of Astur-Leonese is spoken and locally the municipality is called "Santu Michanu".

It is worth taking a detour to Torre de Babia to delve into the depths of this wonderful region. Here you will perfectly understand where the expression "to be in Babia" comes from, which began to be said as a result of the visits that the kings of Leon made here to disconnect.

This mansion, undoubtedly one of the most emblematic in Babia, was about to be transferred to a luxury urbanization in Madrid in the 70s. It was finally bought by Fernando Giejo, who remodeled it and managed to obtain the distinction of national monument . Years later he sold it to the Junta de Castilla y León, which, after a few years of being closed, restored it and turned it into the house of the Babia y Luna park.

faqs.question: What is the difficulty level and terrain like for the El Tajo del Sil circular route?
faqs.answer: This route is classified as a difficult touring bicycle ride, requiring very good fitness. It covers approximately 55.3 kilometers with an elevation gain of around 530 meters. Be prepared for varied terrain, as some segments may be unpaved and challenging to ride.

faqs.question: What type of bicycle is recommended for this route?
faqs.answer: Given the potential for unpaved and difficult segments, a touring bike capable of handling varied surfaces, or even a gravel or mountain bike, would be more suitable than a standard road bike. Cyclists should be prepared for potential rough patches.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete this cycling route?
faqs.answer: The route is estimated to take approximately 3 hours and 50 minutes to complete, though this can vary based on your fitness level and stops along the way.

faqs.question: Where does the route start and where can I park?
faqs.answer: The circular route starts from San Emiliano. While specific parking details aren't provided, San Emiliano is the designated starting point, suggesting parking options would be available within or near the village.

faqs.question: What natural features or landmarks can I see along the way?
faqs.answer: The route offers stunning mountain landscapes of the Babia region. You can also pass by notable highlights such as the San Emiliano Rock Formation, the settlement of Torre de Babia, and the historical site of the Palace of Quiñones.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The Babia region offers picturesque views, but specific seasonal considerations for this route are not detailed. Generally, mountain cycling routes are best enjoyed in spring, summer, and early autumn to avoid harsh winter conditions and ensure clear paths.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or fees required to cycle in the Babia Biosphere Reserve?
faqs.answer: The route is located within the Babia Biosphere Reserve. While there are no specific permits or entrance fees mentioned for cycling this particular route, it's always advisable to check local regulations for the Biosphere Reserve regarding access and activities before your visit.

faqs.question: Is the El Tajo del Sil route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: Information regarding whether this specific cycling route is dog-friendly is not available. When cycling in natural areas, it's generally recommended to keep dogs on a leash to protect wildlife and ensure their safety, especially in a Biosphere Reserve.

faqs.question: Does this route connect with other cycling paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, approximately 4% of this route overlaps with sections of the Transcantábrica trail.

faqs.question: Are there any amenities like cafes or accommodation near the route?
faqs.answer: The route starts from San Emiliano, a village in the Babia region. While specific amenities along the route are not detailed, you would likely find basic services, cafes, or accommodation options in San Emiliano or other nearby villages in the Babia region.

faqs.question: Is this a loop route or an out-and-back?
faqs.answer: This is a circular route, meaning it forms a loop starting and ending in San Emiliano.
